children.short_definition: Temporal data splitting is a dataset partitioning strategy that divides data according to chronological order, using earlier observations for training and later observations for validation or testing. It preserves temporal dependencies and prevents information leakage from future data into model training.

children.name: Feature Leakage
children.short_definition: Feature leakage is a data leakage phenomenon in machine learning where input features contain information that would not be available at prediction time, directly or indirectly encoding the target variable. This leads to overly optimistic model performance during training or evaluation and poor generalization to real-world data.

children.name: Pipeline Encapsulation
children.short_definition: Pipeline encapsulation is the practice of packaging a sequence of data preprocessing, feature transformation, and modeling operations into a single, self-contained unit with a well-defined interface. It promotes modularity, reproducibility, and consistency by managing internal dependencies and execution order while isolating implementation details from external components.

children.name: Preprocessing Isolation
children.short_definition: Preprocessing isolation is the practice of performing data preprocessing steps independently on defined data partitions, such as training, validation, and test sets, to prevent information leakage. It ensures that transformation parameters are learned exclusively from the training set and then applied to validation and test sets, preventing data leakage that would bias model evaluation or compromise generalization assessment.

children.name: Temporal Leakage Control
children.short_definition: Temporal Leakage Control is a process that enforces chronological integrity in data preprocessing, model training, and evaluation to prevent information from future time points from influencing predictions for earlier periods. It is used to ensure valid performance estimates and methodological soundness in time-dependent learning tasks.

children.name: Time-aware Train-Test Splitting
children.short_definition: Time-aware train-test splitting is a data partitioning strategy in which observations are divided into training and testing sets according to their chronological order. It is used to prevent temporal data leakage and to ensure that model evaluation reflects real-world forecasting or sequential prediction conditions.

children.name: Look-Ahead Bias Prevention
children.short_definition: Look-ahead bias prevention refers to methodological practices in data preparation, model training, and evaluation that ensure only information available up to a given time is used, avoiding the inadvertent use of future data. It is essential for producing valid performance estimates and maintaining temporal causality in predictive modeling, particularly for time-dependent data.

children.name: Rolling Window Validation
children.short_definition: Rolling window validation is a time series model evaluation method in which a fixed-length training window is sequentially shifted forward in time, with the model retrained and tested on subsequent observations at each step. It preserves temporal order to assess predictive performance under evolving data distributions.

children.name: Backtesting Procedures
children.short_definition: Backtesting procedures are systematic methods for evaluating a predictive model or algorithm by applying it to historical data to assess its performance under simulated past conditions. They are used to estimate generalization ability and validate model assumptions prior to deployment.

children.name: Feature Timestamp Alignment
children.short_definition: Feature timestamp alignment is the process of associating feature values with the correct reference time relative to a prediction or label event, ensuring that only information available at that time is used. It enforces temporal consistency across features from different sources and prevents the use of future information.

children.name: Purging and Embargo Techniques
children.short_definition: Purging and embargo techniques are data partitioning methods used in time-dependent machine learning to prevent information leakage between training and evaluation sets. Purging removes training samples whose label information overlaps with the test period, while an embargo excludes samples within a specified temporal buffer around the test set to mitigate leakage from serial dependence.

children.name: Causal Ordering Enforcement
children.short_definition: Causal ordering enforcement is the imposition of constraints on data, models, or learning procedures to ensure that variables or events are processed according to their true causal relationships. It prevents information flow from effects to causes, thereby preserving causal validity during training, inference, or evaluation.

children.name: Target Leakage Detection
children.short_definition: Target Leakage Detection is the process of identifying unintended inclusion of information derived from the target variable or future outcomes within model inputs, features, or data preprocessing steps. Its purpose is to prevent artificially inflated performance estimates and ensure the validity and generalizability of predictive models.

children.name: Data Leakage
children.short_definition: Data leakage is the unintended incorporation of information into a machine learning model’s training or evaluation process that would not be available at prediction time. It results in biased performance estimates and can arise from improper data splitting, preprocessing, or feature construction that allows target-related information to influence the model.

children.name: Temporal Leakage
children.short_definition: Temporal leakage is a form of data leakage in which information from future time points is inadvertently used during model training or evaluation relative to the prediction target, violating temporal causality. It results in optimistically biased performance estimates and compromised model validity.

children.name: Train-Test Contamination
children.short_definition: Train-test contamination refers to the unintended leakage of information between training and test datasets, resulting in shared data or derived signals that violate their intended independence. This contamination biases evaluation by inflating performance estimates and undermining the validity of generalization assessments.

children.name: Feature Leakage
children.short_definition: Feature leakage is a data leakage phenomenon in machine learning where input features contain information that would not be available at prediction time, directly or indirectly encoding the target variable. This leads to overly optimistic model performance during training or evaluation and poor generalization to real-world data.

children.name: Proxy Variable Identification
children.short_definition: Proxy Variable Identification is the process of detecting variables in a dataset that indirectly encode or correlate with another attribute of interest, often a sensitive or restricted attribute, enabling indirect inference about it. It is used to assess and mitigate unintended information leakage or bias in statistical and machine learning models.

children.name: Post-Outcome Feature Detection
children.short_definition: Post-Outcome Feature Detection is the process of identifying features whose values are determined, recorded, or become available after the target outcome has occurred, based on temporal ordering. It is used to detect target leakage (also called data leakage or leakage) and ensure temporal validity of features in machine learning models to prevent overly optimistic performance estimates.

children.name: Cross-Validation Leakage
children.short_definition: Cross-validation leakage is a form of data leakage that occurs when information from the validation or test folds is inadvertently used during model training or preprocessing, leading to overly optimistic performance estimates. It typically arises from improper data splitting, shared preprocessing steps, or feature engineering performed before cross-validation.

children.name: Feature Leakage Analysis
children.short_definition: Feature Leakage Analysis is the process of identifying and evaluating features that inadvertently contain information from the target variable or data unavailable at prediction time, which can bias model training and evaluation. Its objective is to detect and mitigate such leakage to ensure valid, reliable, and generalizable model performance.

children.name: Data Leakage Detection
children.short_definition: Data leakage detection is the process of identifying unintended exposure of information from the test or validation datasets, or future information not available at prediction time, into model training workflows that can artificially inflate performance estimates. It aims to ensure that learned patterns and evaluation metrics reflect only information legitimately available at the time predictions would be made in deployment.

children.name: Train-Test Contamination
children.short_definition: Train-test contamination refers to the unintended leakage of information between training and test datasets, resulting in shared data or derived signals that violate their intended independence. This contamination biases evaluation by inflating performance estimates and undermining the validity of generalization assessments.

children.name: Temporal Leakage
children.short_definition: Temporal leakage is a form of data leakage in which information from future time points is inadvertently used during model training or evaluation relative to the prediction target, violating temporal causality. It results in optimistically biased performance estimates and compromised model validity.

children.name: Target Leakage
children.short_definition: Target leakage is a form of data leakage in which input features contain information derived from the target variable or from future data unavailable at prediction time, leading to biased model training and overly optimistic performance estimates. It typically arises from improper feature construction, labeling, or data partitioning.

children.name: Feature Engineering Validation
children.short_definition: Feature engineering validation is the process of verifying that engineered features are correctly constructed, statistically sound, and do not introduce data leakage or bias. It evaluates feature stability and predictive contribution across appropriate training, validation, and test data to ensure reliable model performance.

children.name: Cross-Validation Integrity
children.short_definition: Cross-validation integrity refers to the correctness and rigor with which data partitions are constructed and used during cross-validation to ensure that training and evaluation sets are strictly independent. It aims to prevent information leakage, sampling bias, or protocol violations that could lead to overly optimistic or invalid performance estimates.

children.name: Pipeline Isolation
children.short_definition: Pipeline isolation is the practice of separating machine learning pipeline components, data flows, and execution environments to prevent interference, data leakage, and unintended side effects. It enforces well-defined boundaries and interfaces to improve reproducibility, security, and operational reliability.

children.name: Pipeline Isolation
children.short_definition: Pipeline isolation is the process of separating the components, execution environments, or stages of an artificial intelligence or data processing pipeline to prevent unintended interactions, side effects, or cross-contamination. It is used to improve reproducibility, reliability, security, and fault containment during model development and deployment.

children.name: Containerization
children.short_definition: Containerization is a software virtualization approach that packages applications, their dependencies, and configuration into isolated, lightweight containers that share a common operating system kernel. It enables consistent execution, portability, and reproducibility of data processing and machine learning workflows across different computing environments.

children.name: Environment Management
children.short_definition: Environment management is the practice of controlling and maintaining the computational, software, and data settings in which machine learning systems are developed, trained, tested, and deployed. It ensures consistency, reproducibility, and isolation of experiments across different stages and platforms.

children.name: Dependency Isolation
children.short_definition: Dependency isolation is the practice of encapsulating and controlling software dependencies so that their versions and configurations do not conflict with those of other systems or processes. In machine learning workflows, it enables reproducible, stable execution of data processing and model training by ensuring consistent dependency environments.

children.name: Resource Isolation
children.short_definition: Resource isolation is the enforcement of strict boundaries that partition compute, memory, storage, and network resources among processes or workloads to prevent mutual interference. It ensures predictable performance, security, and fault containment in shared computing environments.

children.name: Reproducibility
children.short_definition: Reproducibility is the property that experimental results can be consistently obtained by the same or independent researchers using the same data, methods, and computational conditions. Replicability refers to obtaining consistent findings when independent researchers use different data or implementations to test the same hypothesis. Together, these ensure that reported findings are verifiable and reliable.

children.name: Security Sandboxing
children.short_definition: Security sandboxing is a security mechanism that isolates code, processes, or data within a controlled execution environment to restrict access to system resources and prevent unintended interactions with other components. It is used to analyze, process, or execute potentially untrusted data or models while minimizing the risk of compromise, data leakage, or system-wide impact.

children.name: Multi-Tenancy Separation
children.short_definition: Multi-tenancy separation is the set of architectural and operational mechanisms that ensure data, models, and computational resources belonging to different tenants in a shared system are logically or physically isolated to prevent unauthorized access, interference, or information leakage. It is a critical property for maintaining security, privacy, and compliance in multi-tenant machine learning platforms.

children.name: Validation Strategy Review
children.short_definition: Validation Strategy Review is a process for systematically assessing the design, appropriateness, and rigor of methods used to validate a model’s performance, generalization, and reliability. It evaluates whether validation procedures, assumptions, and metrics align with the problem context, data characteristics, and intended use.

children.name: Cross-Validation
children.short_definition: Cross-validation is a resampling methodology used to evaluate a model’s generalization performance by partitioning a dataset into complementary training and validation subsets across multiple iterations. It provides an estimate of predictive performance and helps mitigate overfitting by ensuring evaluation on unseen data.

children.name: Train-Validation-Test Split
children.short_definition: A train-validation-test split is a dataset partitioning strategy in which data are divided into three disjoint subsets used respectively for model fitting, hyperparameter tuning or model selection, and final unbiased performance evaluation.

children.name: Holdout Validation
children.short_definition: Holdout validation is a model evaluation method in which a dataset is partitioned into disjoint training and validation or test subsets, with the model trained on one subset and evaluated on the held-out data to estimate generalization performance.

children.name: Stratified Sampling
children.short_definition: Stratified sampling is a data sampling technique in which a population is divided into distinct subgroups based on specific characteristics, and samples are drawn from each subgroup in proportion to their representation. It is used to ensure that the sampled dataset accurately reflects the distribution of key attributes in the overall population.

children.name: Temporal Validation
children.short_definition: Temporal validation is a model evaluation approach in which data are split according to their chronological order, ensuring that training is performed on earlier time periods and validation on subsequent periods to prevent information leakage across time.

children.name: Hyperparameter Tuning Protocols
children.short_definition: Hyperparameter tuning protocols are systematic procedures that define how hyperparameters of a machine learning model are selected, explored, and evaluated during training. They specify the search strategy, evaluation methodology, and stopping criteria used to optimize model performance while controlling for computational cost and generalization.

children.name: Data Leakage Detection
children.short_definition: Data leakage detection is the process of identifying unintended exposure of information from the test or validation datasets into model training workflows, or future information into past predictions, that can artificially inflate performance estimates. It aims to ensure that learned patterns and evaluation metrics reflect only information legitimately available at prediction time.

children.name: Performance Metrics Selection
children.short_definition: Performance metrics selection is the process of choosing appropriate quantitative measures to evaluate and compare the performance of machine learning models, based on the task objectives, data characteristics, and operational constraints. It ensures that model assessment aligns with the intended use, optimization goals, and potential trade-offs among different aspects of performance.

children.name: Supervised Learning
children.short_definition: Supervised learning is a machine learning paradigm in which a model is trained on labeled data, learning a mapping from input features to known output targets. The objective is to generalize this mapping to accurately predict outputs for unseen inputs.

children.name: Linear Models
children.short_definition: Linear models are a class of statistical and machine learning models that represent the relationship between input features and an output as a linear combination of the features and learned parameters. They are widely used for regression and classification tasks due to their simplicity, interpretability, and computational efficiency.

children.name: Linear Regression
children.short_definition: Linear regression is a statistical and machine learning method that models the relationship between a dependent variable and one or more independent variables by fitting a linear function that minimizes the sum of squared residuals. It is commonly used for prediction and for estimating the strength and direction of linear associations between variables.

children.name: Regression
children.short_definition: Regression is a supervised learning paradigm that models the relationship between one or more independent variables and a continuous dependent variable by estimating a function that minimizes prediction error. It is commonly used for prediction, forecasting, and quantifying variable relationships.

children.name: Mean Squared Error (MSE)
children.short_definition: Mean Squared Error (MSE) is a loss function that measures the average of the squared differences between predicted values and corresponding true values in a dataset. It quantifies prediction error magnitude by penalizing larger errors more heavily due to squaring.

children.name: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)
children.short_definition: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) is a metric that quantifies the average magnitude of prediction errors by taking the square root of the mean of squared differences between predicted and observed values. It is expressed in the same units as the target variable and is sensitive to large errors.

children.name: Mean Absolute Error (MAE)
children.short_definition: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) is a regression evaluation metric that measures the average of the absolute differences between predicted values and true values. It quantifies prediction accuracy by expressing errors in the same units as the target variable.

children.name: R² (Coefficient of Determination)
children.short_definition: The coefficient of determination (R²) is a statistical measure that quantifies the proportion of variance in a dependent variable that is explained by an independent variable or model. It provides an indication of model fit by assessing how well observed outcomes are replicated by the model’s predictions.

children.name: Adjusted R² (if applicable)
children.short_definition: Adjusted R² is a statistical measure of goodness of fit for regression models that modifies the coefficient of determination (R²) to account for the number of predictors relative to the sample size. It penalizes the inclusion of unnecessary variables, providing a more reliable estimate of explanatory power when comparing models with different numbers of predictors.

children.name: MAPE / SMAPE (if applicable)
children.short_definition: MAPE (Mean Absolute Percentage Error) and SMAPE (Symmetric Mean Absolute Percentage Error) are scale-independent error metrics used to evaluate the accuracy of forecasting and regression models by measuring relative prediction errors as percentages. SMAPE modifies MAPE by symmetrizing the denominator to reduce bias and instability when actual values are near zero.

children.name: Logistic Regression
children.short_definition: Logistic regression is a statistical and machine learning method for modeling the probability of a binary or categorical outcome by applying a logistic (sigmoid) function to a linear combination of input features. It estimates model parameters by maximizing the likelihood of the observed data, typically using maximum likelihood estimation.

children.name: Classification
children.short_definition: Classification is a supervised learning task in which a model assigns input instances to one of a finite set of predefined categories based on their features. It involves learning decision boundaries or probabilistic mappings from labeled training data.

children.name: Accuracy
children.short_definition: Accuracy is a performance metric that measures the proportion of correct predictions made by a model out of the total number of predictions. It is commonly used in classification tasks but can be unreliable when class distributions are highly imbalanced.

children.name: Confusion matrix
children.short_definition: A confusion matrix is a tabular representation of a classification model’s performance that cross-tabulates predicted labels against true labels to quantify correct and incorrect predictions. It serves as the basis for deriving evaluation metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score.

children.name: Precision
children.short_definition: Precision is a performance metric in classification tasks that measures the proportion of predicted positive instances that are correctly identified as positive. It quantifies the accuracy of positive predictions by comparing true positives to the total number of positive predictions.

children.name: Recall
children.short_definition: Recall is a performance metric in classification that measures the proportion of actual positive instances correctly identified by a model, calculated as the ratio of true positives to the sum of true positives and false negatives. It reflects a model’s ability to capture all relevant instances of a target class.

children.name: Sensitivity
children.short_definition: Sensitivity is a performance metric in classification that measures the proportion of actual positive instances correctly identified by a model, also known as the true positive rate or recall.

children.name: Specificity
children.short_definition: Specificity is an evaluation metric in classification tasks that measures the proportion of actual negative instances correctly identified by a model, defined as the true negative rate equal to the number of true negatives divided by the sum of true negatives and false positives.

children.name: F1 score
children.short_definition: The F1 score is the harmonic mean of precision and recall, providing a single performance metric that balances false positive and false negative errors in classification tasks.

children.name: Log loss
children.short_definition: Log loss, also known as logarithmic loss or cross-entropy loss, is a probabilistic loss function that measures the negative log-likelihood of the true labels given predicted class probabilities. It penalizes confident incorrect predictions more heavily and is commonly used to evaluate and train probabilistic classification models.

children.name: Binary cross-entropy
children.short_definition: Binary cross-entropy is a loss function that quantifies the difference between true binary labels and predicted probabilities by computing the negative log-likelihood of a Bernoulli distribution.

children.name: ROC curve
children.short_definition: A ROC curve, or Receiver Operating Characteristic curve, is a graphical representation of a binary classifier’s performance that plots the true positive rate against the false positive rate across varying decision thresholds. It characterizes the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity and is commonly used to assess discriminatory ability independent of class distribution.

children.name: AUC
children.short_definition: AUC, or Area Under the Curve, is a scalar performance metric that summarizes the area under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve, quantifying a model’s ability to discriminate between classes across all decision thresholds.

children.name: Macro-averaged
children.short_definition: Macro-averaged refers to an evaluation approach in which a performance metric is computed independently for each class and then averaged with equal weight across all classes, regardless of their frequency. It is commonly used in multiclass or multilabel classification to assess overall performance while treating all classes as equally important.

children.name: Micro-averaged
children.short_definition: Micro-averaged refers to an evaluation approach in multi-class or multi-label classification where performance metrics are computed by aggregating true positives, false positives, and false negatives across all classes before calculation, giving equal weight to each individual instance.

children.name: Weighted-averaged
children.short_definition: Weighted-averaged refers to a method of combining multiple values into a single aggregate by assigning each value a specified weight that reflects its relative importance, with the result computed as the sum of weighted values divided by the sum of weights.

children.name: Ridge / Lasso / Elastic Net
children.short_definition: Ridge, Lasso, and Elastic Net are regularization techniques for linear models that add penalty terms to the loss function to control model complexity and reduce overfitting. Ridge uses an L2 penalty, Lasso uses an L1 penalty that can induce sparsity in coefficients, and Elastic Net combines both L1 and L2 penalties to balance shrinkage and variable selection.

children.short_definition: Hinge loss is a convex loss function used primarily in margin-based classifiers, defined as the maximum of zero and one minus the product of the true label and the predicted score. children.short_definition: Tree-based Models are algorithms that learn decision rules by recursively splitting data into hierarchical tree structures.

children.name: Decision Trees
children.short_definition: Decision trees are supervised learning models that represent decision-making processes as a hierarchical structure of nodes, where internal nodes correspond to feature-based tests and leaf nodes represent predicted outcomes. They are used for classification and regression by recursively partitioning the input space to minimize impurity or prediction error.

children.name: Feature Importance
children.short_definition: Feature importance is a measure of the contribution of individual input variables to a predictive model’s output, typically quantified based on their influence on model performance or decision-making. It is used to assess which features are most relevant in explaining or predicting the target variable.

children.name: Gini Importance
children.short_definition: Gini Importance is a feature importance measure used in decision tree–based models that quantifies the total reduction in Gini impurity contributed by a feature across all splits in which it is used, typically weighted by the number of samples reaching each split. It reflects how much a feature contributes to improving node purity during tree construction.

children.name: Permutation Importance
children.short_definition: Permutation importance is a model-agnostic method for estimating feature importance by randomly permuting the values of a feature and measuring the resulting change in a specified performance metric. A larger decrease in performance indicates greater dependence of the model on that feature.

children.name: Gradient Boosted Trees
children.short_definition: Gradient Boosted Trees are an ensemble learning method that builds a predictive model by sequentially adding decision trees, where each new tree is trained to minimize the errors of the combined ensemble using gradient-based optimization of a specified loss function. The approach integrates boosting with decision trees to achieve high predictive accuracy and robustness for both regression and classification tasks.

children.name: Ranking
children.short_definition: Ranking is the process of ordering a set of items according to a defined criterion or learned scoring function that reflects relative relevance, preference, or importance. In artificial intelligence, ranking typically involves models that infer relative orderings from data rather than predicting absolute values or class labels.

children.name: NDCG
children.short_definition: Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG) is a ranking evaluation metric that measures the usefulness of an ordered list of results by comparing the discounted cumulative gain of the ranking to that of an ideal ranking. It accounts for the graded relevance of items and applies logarithmic discounting to penalize lower-ranked relevant items.

children.name: MAP
children.short_definition: MAP (Maximum A Posteriori) is a statistical estimation principle that selects the parameter or hypothesis with the highest posterior probability given observed data, combining prior knowledge with the likelihood. It is commonly used in Bayesian inference to balance prior assumptions and empirical evidence.

children.name: Precision@k
children.short_definition: Precision@k is an evaluation metric in information retrieval and recommendation systems that measures the proportion of relevant items among the top k retrieved or ranked results for a given query or user.

children.name: Ensemble Methods
children.short_definition: Ensemble methods are machine learning techniques that combine multiple predictive models to produce a single aggregated output, aiming to improve accuracy, robustness, and generalization compared to individual models. They leverage diversity among constituent models to reduce variance, bias, or both.

children.name: Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating)
children.short_definition: Bagging, or bootstrap aggregating, is an ensemble learning technique that trains multiple models on different bootstrap samples of the training data and combines their predictions to improve generalization. It primarily reduces model variance and increases robustness, especially for high-variance learners.

children.name: Classification
children.short_definition: Classification is a supervised learning task in which a model assigns input instances to one of a finite set of predefined categories based on their features. It involves learning decision boundaries or probabilistic mappings from labeled training data.

children.name: Accuracy
children.short_definition: Accuracy is a performance metric that measures the proportion of correct predictions made by a model out of the total number of predictions. It is commonly used in classification tasks but can be unreliable when class distributions are highly imbalanced.

children.name: Confusion matrix
children.short_definition: A confusion matrix is a tabular representation of a classification model’s performance that cross-tabulates predicted labels against true labels to quantify correct and incorrect predictions. It serves as the basis for deriving evaluation metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score.

children.name: Precision
children.short_definition: Precision is a performance metric in classification tasks that measures the proportion of predicted positive instances that are correctly identified as positive. It quantifies the accuracy of positive predictions by comparing true positives to the total number of positive predictions.

children.name: Recall / Sensitivity
children.short_definition: Recall, also known as sensitivity or true positive rate, is a performance metric that measures the proportion of actual positive instances correctly identified by a model. It is defined as the ratio of true positives to the sum of true positives and false negatives.

children.name: Specificity
children.short_definition: Specificity is an evaluation metric in classification tasks that measures the proportion of actual negative instances correctly identified by a model, defined as the true negative rate equal to the number of true negatives divided by the sum of true negatives and false positives.

children.name: F1 score
children.short_definition: The F1 score is the harmonic mean of precision and recall, providing a single performance metric that balances false positive and false negative errors in classification tasks.

children.name: ROC curve
children.short_definition: A ROC curve, or Receiver Operating Characteristic curve, is a graphical representation of a binary classifier’s performance that plots the true positive rate against the false positive rate across varying decision thresholds. It characterizes the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity and is commonly used to assess discriminatory ability independent of class distribution.

children.name: AUC
children.short_definition: AUC, or Area Under the Curve, is a scalar performance metric that summarizes the area under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve, quantifying a model’s ability to discriminate between classes across all decision thresholds.

children.name: Log loss
children.short_definition: Log loss, also known as logarithmic loss or cross-entropy loss, is a probabilistic loss function that measures the negative log-likelihood of the true labels given predicted class probabilities. It penalizes confident incorrect predictions more heavily and is commonly used to evaluate and train probabilistic classification models.

children.name: Regression
children.short_definition: Regression is a supervised learning paradigm that models the relationship between one or more independent variables and a continuous dependent variable by estimating a function that minimizes prediction error. It is commonly used for prediction, forecasting, and quantifying variable relationships.

children.name: Mean Squared Error (MSE)
children.short_definition: Mean Squared Error (MSE) is a loss function that measures the average of the squared differences between predicted values and corresponding true values in a dataset. It quantifies prediction error magnitude by penalizing larger errors more heavily due to squaring.

children.name: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)
children.short_definition: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) is a metric that quantifies the average magnitude of prediction errors by taking the square root of the mean of squared differences between predicted and observed values. It is expressed in the same units as the target variable and is sensitive to large errors.

children.name: Mean Absolute Error (MAE)
children.short_definition: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) is a regression evaluation metric that measures the average of the absolute differences between predicted values and true values. It quantifies prediction accuracy by expressing errors in the same units as the target variable.

children.name: R² (Coefficient of Determination)
children.short_definition: The coefficient of determination (R²) is a statistical measure that quantifies the proportion of variance in a dependent variable that is explained by an independent variable or model. It provides an indication of model fit by assessing how well observed outcomes are replicated by the model’s predictions.

children.name: Boosting
children.short_definition: Boosting is an ensemble learning technique in which multiple weak learners are trained sequentially, each focusing on correcting the errors of its predecessors, and then combined into a single strong predictive model. The method typically adjusts training weights or sampling to emphasize previously misclassified instances.

children.name: Ranking
children.short_definition: Ranking is the process of ordering a set of items according to a defined criterion or learned scoring function that reflects relative relevance, preference, or importance. In artificial intelligence, ranking typically involves models that infer relative orderings from data rather than predicting absolute values or class labels.

children.name: NDCG
children.short_definition: Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG) is a ranking evaluation metric that measures the usefulness of an ordered list of results by comparing the discounted cumulative gain of the ranking to that of an ideal ranking. It accounts for the graded relevance of items and applies logarithmic discounting to penalize lower-ranked relevant items.

children.name: MAP
children.short_definition: MAP (Maximum A Posteriori) is a statistical estimation principle that selects the parameter or hypothesis with the highest posterior probability given observed data, combining prior knowledge with the likelihood. It is commonly used in Bayesian inference to balance prior assumptions and empirical evidence.

children.name: Precision@k
children.short_definition: Precision@k is an evaluation metric in information retrieval and recommendation systems that measures the proportion of relevant items among the top k retrieved or ranked results for a given query or user.

children.name: Stacking (Stacked Generalization)
children.short_definition: Stacking, or stacked generalization, is an ensemble learning technique in which multiple base models are trained on the same dataset and their outputs are used as inputs to a higher-level meta-model that learns how to best combine their predictions to improve overall performance.

children.name: Voting Ensembles

They aim to improve predictive performance and robustness by leveraging the collective decision of diverse models.

children.name: Kernel-based Models
children.short_definition: Kernel-based models are a class of machine learning algorithms that use kernel functions to implicitly map input data into high-dimensional feature spaces, enabling the modeling of nonlinear relationships using linear methods in those spaces. They rely on inner products defined by kernels to operate without explicitly computing the feature transformations.

children.name: Kernel SVM (Non-linear SVM)
children.short_definition: A Kernel SVM, or non-linear Support Vector Machine, is a supervised learning model that uses a kernel function to implicitly map input data into a higher-dimensional feature space where a maximum-margin separating hyperplane can be learned. This approach enables the modeling of non-linear decision boundaries without explicitly computing the feature transformation.

children.name: Hinge loss
children.short_definition: Hinge loss is a convex loss function used primarily in margin-based classifiers, defined as the maximum of zero and one minus the product of the true label and the predicted score. It penalizes predictions that are incorrect or within the margin, thereby encouraging a large separation between classes.

children.name: Macro-averaged
children.short_definition: Macro-averaged refers to an evaluation approach in which a performance metric is computed independently for each class and then averaged with equal weight across all classes, regardless of their frequency. It is commonly used in multiclass or multilabel classification to assess overall performance while treating all classes as equally important.

children.name: Micro-averaged
children.short_definition: Micro-averaged refers to an evaluation approach in multi-class or multi-label classification where performance metrics are computed by aggregating true positives, false positives, and false negatives across all classes before calculation, giving equal weight to each individual instance.

children.name: Weighted-averaged
children.short_definition: Weighted-averaged refers to a method of combining multiple values into a single aggregate by assigning each value a specified weight that reflects its relative importance, with the result computed as the sum of weighted values divided by the sum of weights.

children.name: Epsilon-insensitive loss
children.short_definition: Epsilon-insensitive loss is a loss function used primarily in support vector regression that ignores prediction errors within a specified margin ε and penalizes only deviations exceeding this threshold. It promotes model robustness by allowing small errors without incurring loss, thereby encouraging sparsity in the regression solution.

It seeks a function with maximum generalization by balancing model complexity and prediction error through convex optimization.

children.name: Instance-based Methods
children.short_definition: Instance-based methods are a class of machine learning algorithms that make predictions by directly comparing new inputs to stored training instances, rather than learning an explicit global model. They typically rely on similarity or distance measures to infer outputs from the most relevant stored examples.

children.name: k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN)

It performs no explicit training, deferring computation until prediction time.

children.short_definition: A confusion matrix is a tabular representation of a classification model’s performance that cross-tabulates predicted labels against true labels to quantify correct and incorrect predictions. It serves as the basis for deriving evaluation metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score.

children.name: Precision
children.short_definition: Precision is a performance metric in classification tasks that measures the proportion of predicted positive instances that are correctly identified as positive. It quantifies the accuracy of positive predictions by comparing true positives to the total number of positive predictions.

children.name: Recall / Sensitivity
children.short_definition: Recall, also known as sensitivity or true positive rate, is a performance metric that measures the proportion of actual positive instances correctly identified by a model. It is defined as the ratio of true positives to the sum of true positives and false negatives.

children.name: Specificity
children.short_definition: Specificity is an evaluation metric in classification tasks that measures the proportion of actual negative instances correctly identified by a model, defined as the true negative rate equal to the number of true negatives divided by the sum of true negatives and false positives.

children.name: F1 score
children.short_definition: The F1 score is the harmonic mean of precision and recall, providing a single performance metric that balances false positive and false negative errors in classification tasks.

children.name: ROC curve
children.short_definition: A ROC curve, or Receiver Operating Characteristic curve, is a graphical representation of a binary classifier’s performance that plots the true positive rate against the false positive rate across varying decision thresholds. It characterizes the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity and is commonly used to assess discriminatory ability independent of class distribution.

children.name: AUC
children.short_definition: AUC, or Area Under the Curve, is a scalar performance metric that summarizes the area under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve, quantifying a model’s ability to discriminate between classes across all decision thresholds.

children.name: Regression
children.short_definition: Regression is a supervised learning paradigm that models the relationship between one or more independent variables and a continuous dependent variable by estimating a function that minimizes prediction error. It is commonly used for prediction, forecasting, and quantifying variable relationships.

children.name: Mean Squared Error (MSE)
children.short_definition: Mean Squared Error (MSE) is a loss function that measures the average of the squared differences between predicted values and corresponding true values in a dataset. It quantifies prediction error magnitude by penalizing larger errors more heavily due to squaring.

children.name: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)
children.short_definition: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) is a metric that quantifies the average magnitude of prediction errors by taking the square root of the mean of squared differences between predicted and observed values. It is expressed in the same units as the target variable and is sensitive to large errors.

children.name: Mean Absolute Error (MAE)
children.short_definition: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) is a regression evaluation metric that measures the average of the absolute differences between predicted values and true values. It quantifies prediction accuracy by expressing errors in the same units as the target variable.

children.name: R² (Coefficient of Determination)
children.short_definition: The coefficient of determination (R²) is a statistical measure that quantifies the proportion of variance in a dependent variable that is explained by an independent variable or model. It provides an indication of model fit by assessing how well observed outcomes are replicated by the model’s predictions.

children.name: Weighted k-Nearest Neighbors
children.short_definition: Weighted k-Nearest Neighbors is a variant of the k-nearest neighbors algorithm in which the contribution of each neighbor to the prediction is scaled by a weight, typically based on distance or similarity to the query point. This weighting scheme reduces the influence of more distant neighbors and can improve predictive accuracy compared to uniform voting or averaging.

children.short_definition: Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) is a generative probabilistic model for discrete data that represents documents as mixtures of latent topics, where each topic is characterized by a probability distribution over words. It assumes Dirichlet priors over document–topic and topic–word distributions and is commonly used for unsupervised topic modeling.

children.name: Topic Modeling
children.short_definition: Topic modeling is an unsupervised statistical technique in natural language processing that discovers latent thematic structures in a collection of documents by identifying patterns of word co-occurrence. It represents documents as mixtures of topics and topics as probability distributions over terms.

children.name: Perplexity
children.short_definition: Perplexity is a metric for evaluating probabilistic models, particularly language models, defined as the exponential of the average negative log-likelihood (cross-entropy) of a dataset under the model. Lower perplexity indicates greater predictive confidence and better model fit to the data.

children.name: Topic Coherence
children.short_definition: Topic coherence is a quantitative measure of the semantic consistency of a topic, typically computed from the degree of co-occurrence or semantic relatedness among its highest-probability terms within a reference corpus. It is used to evaluate the interpretability and quality of topics generated by topic modeling algorithms.

children.name: Neural Networks
children.short_definition: Neural networks are computational models composed of interconnected layers of artificial neurons that process information through weighted connections and nonlinear activation functions. children.name: Multilayer Perceptron (MLP)
children.short_definition: A multilayer perceptron (MLP) is a feedforward artificial neural network composed of an input layer, one or more hidden layers, and an output layer of interconnected neurons with nonlinear activation functions. It is typically trained using gradient-based optimization methods, such as backpropagation, to learn complex nonlinear mappings between inputs and outputs.

It is commonly used for prediction, forecasting, and quantifying variable relationships.

children.name: Mean Squared Error (MSE)
children.short_definition: Mean Squared Error (MSE) is a loss function that measures the average of the squared differences between predicted values and corresponding true values in a dataset. It quantifies prediction error magnitude by penalizing larger errors more heavily due to squaring.

children.name: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)
children.short_definition: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) is a metric that quantifies the average magnitude of prediction errors by taking the square root of the mean of squared differences between predicted and observed values. It is expressed in the same units as the target variable and is sensitive to large errors.

children.name: Mean Absolute Error (MAE)
children.short_definition: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) is a regression evaluation metric that measures the average of the absolute differences between predicted values and true values. It quantifies prediction accuracy by expressing errors in the same units as the target variable.

children.name: R² (Coefficient of Determination)
children.short_definition: The coefficient of determination (R²) is a statistical measure that quantifies the proportion of variance in a dependent variable that is explained by an independent variable or model. It provides an indication of model fit by assessing how well observed outcomes are replicated by the model’s predictions.

children.name: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)
children.short_definition: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are a class of deep neural networks designed to process grid-structured data by applying convolutional operations with shared weights and local receptive fields. They learn hierarchical representations through stacked convolutional, nonlinear, and pooling layers, enabling efficient feature extraction and pattern recognition.

children.name: Classification
children.short_definition: Classification is a supervised learning task in which a model assigns input instances to one of a finite set of predefined categories based on their features. It involves learning decision boundaries or probabilistic mappings from labeled training data.

children.name: Accuracy
children.short_definition: Accuracy is a performance metric that measures the proportion of correct predictions made by a model out of the total number of predictions. It is commonly used in classification tasks but can be unreliable when class distributions are highly imbalanced.

children.name: Precision
children.short_definition: Precision is a performance metric in classification tasks that measures the proportion of predicted positive instances that are correctly identified as positive. It quantifies the accuracy of positive predictions by comparing true positives to the total number of positive predictions.

children.name: Recall / Sensitivity
children.short_definition: Recall, also known as sensitivity or true positive rate, is a performance metric that measures the proportion of actual positive instances correctly identified by a model. It is defined as the ratio of true positives to the sum of true positives and false negatives.

children.name: F1 score
children.short_definition: The F1 score is the harmonic mean of precision and recall, providing a single performance metric that balances false positive and false negative errors in classification tasks.

children.name: Top-k Accuracy
children.short_definition: Top-k accuracy is an evaluation metric for classification models that measures the proportion of instances in which the true label appears among the model’s k highest-scoring predicted classes. It is commonly used when multiple plausible predictions are acceptable or when class ambiguity is present.

children.name: ROC curve
children.short_definition: A ROC curve, or Receiver Operating Characteristic curve, is a graphical representation of a binary classifier’s performance that plots the true positive rate against the false positive rate across varying decision thresholds. It characterizes the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity and is commonly used to assess discriminatory ability independent of class distribution.

children.name: AUC
children.short_definition: AUC, or Area Under the Curve, is a scalar performance metric that summarizes the area under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve, quantifying a model’s ability to discriminate between classes across all decision thresholds.

children.name: Object Detection
children.short_definition: Object detection is a computer vision task that involves identifying and localizing instances of predefined object classes within an image or video, typically by predicting bounding boxes and associated class labels. It combines elements of image classification and spatial localization to determine both what objects are present and where they appear.

children.name: Intersection over Union (IoU)
children.short_definition: Intersection over Union (IoU) is a similarity metric that quantifies the overlap between two spatial regions by computing the ratio of the area of their intersection to the area of their union. It is commonly used to evaluate the accuracy of predicted regions against reference regions in computer vision tasks.

children.name: mean Average Precision (mAP)
children.short_definition: Mean Average Precision (mAP) is an evaluation metric for information retrieval and detection systems that computes the mean of average precision scores across multiple queries, classes, or categories. It summarizes both precision and recall by integrating precision over recall levels, providing a single scalar measure of ranking or detection performance.

children.name: Precision
children.short_definition: Precision is a performance metric in classification tasks that measures the proportion of predicted positive instances that are correctly identified as positive. It quantifies the accuracy of positive predictions by comparing true positives to the total number of positive predictions.

children.name: Recall
children.short_definition: Recall is a performance metric in classification that measures the proportion of actual positive instances correctly identified by a model, calculated as the ratio of true positives to the sum of true positives and false negatives. It reflects a model’s ability to capture all relevant instances of a target class.

children.name: Image Segmentation
children.short_definition: Image segmentation is the process of partitioning a digital image into multiple disjoint regions or segments such that pixels within each region share similar visual or semantic characteristics. It is commonly used to simplify image representation and to enable object-level analysis in computer vision tasks.

children.name: Dice Coefficient
children.short_definition: The Dice coefficient, also known as the Sørensen–Dice coefficient, is a statistical similarity measure that quantifies the overlap between two sets as twice the size of their intersection divided by the sum of their sizes. It is commonly used to evaluate agreement between binary data, particularly in image segmentation and information retrieval.

children.name: Jaccard Index (IoU)
children.short_definition: The Jaccard Index, also known as Intersection over Union (IoU), is a similarity metric defined as the ratio of the size of the intersection of two sets to the size of their union. It is commonly used to quantify overlap between predicted and ground truth regions in tasks such as image segmentation and object detection.

children.name: Pixel Accuracy
children.short_definition: Pixel accuracy is an evaluation metric for image segmentation and classification tasks that measures the proportion of pixels whose predicted labels exactly match the ground truth labels across an image or dataset. It provides a simple overall measure of classification correctness at the pixel level.

children.name: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN)
children.short_definition: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) are a class of neural networks designed to model sequential data by maintaining internal hidden states that capture temporal dependencies across time steps. They process inputs iteratively, allowing information from previous elements in a sequence to influence current outputs.

children.name: Sequence Classification
children.short_definition: Sequence classification is a supervised learning task that assigns a discrete label to an entire ordered sequence by modeling dependencies and structure across its elements.

children.name: Sequence Prediction
children.short_definition: Sequence prediction is the task of modeling and inferring future elements of an ordered sequence based on observed past elements, typically by learning temporal dependencies and probabilistic relationships within the data. It is a fundamental problem in machine learning, time-series analysis, and sequential modeling.

children.name: Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)
children.short_definition: Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) is a recurrent neural network architecture that incorporates a memory cell and gating mechanisms to regulate information flow over time. It is designed to capture long-range temporal dependencies and mitigate the vanishing and exploding gradient problems during sequence learning.

children.name: Time Series Forecasting
children.short_definition: Time series forecasting is the task of modeling and predicting future values of a temporally ordered sequence based on its historical patterns, trends, and temporal dependencies. It typically involves statistical or machine learning methods that account for time-dependent structure such as seasonality, autocorrelation, and non-stationarity.

children.name: Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)
children.short_definition: A Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) is a type of recurrent neural network architecture designed to model sequential data by using gating mechanisms to control the flow of information and mitigate vanishing gradient problems. It combines update and reset gates to adaptively retain or discard past information with fewer parameters than long short-term memory networks.

children.name: Autoencoders
children.short_definition: Autoencoders are a class of unsupervised neural networks designed to learn compact, latent representations of input data by encoding it into a lower-dimensional space and then reconstructing it as accurately as possible. They are commonly used for dimensionality reduction, feature learning, and data denoising.

children.name: Dimensionality Reduction
children.short_definition: Dimensionality reduction is the process of transforming high-dimensional data into a lower-dimensional representation while preserving essential information or structure. It is commonly used to reduce computational complexity, mitigate the effects of high dimensionality, and support efficient analysis and learning.

children.name: Reconstruction Error (MSE)
children.short_definition: Reconstruction error measured by mean squared error (MSE) is a quantitative metric that computes the average of the squared differences between original input data and its reconstructed output from a model. It is commonly used to assess how accurately a model preserves information during encoding and decoding processes.

children.name: Reconstruction Error (MAE)
children.short_definition: Reconstruction Error (MAE) is a metric that quantifies the average absolute difference between an original input and its reconstructed version produced by a model. It measures reconstruction fidelity by computing the mean of element-wise absolute errors across all input dimensions.

children.name: Anomaly Detection
children.short_definition: Anomaly detection is the task of identifying data points, sequences, or patterns that deviate significantly from an expected model or distribution of normal behavior. It employs statistical, machine learning, or signal processing methods to characterize normality and detect deviations.

children.name: State Space Models
children.short_definition: State space models are mathematical frameworks for modeling dynamical systems in which an unobserved latent state evolves over time according to a state transition process and generates observable data through an observation model. They are commonly used to represent temporal dependencies and uncertainty in sequential data.

children.name: Kalman Filter
children.short_definition: A Kalman filter is a recursive Bayesian estimation algorithm that computes the optimal linear minimum mean-square error estimate of a system’s state by combining a dynamic state-space model with noisy observations. It operates through sequential prediction and update steps under the assumption of linear dynamics and Gaussian noise.

children.name: State Estimation
children.short_definition: State estimation is the process of inferring the internal, often unobserved, state of a dynamic system from partial and noisy observations using a mathematical model of system dynamics and measurement processes. It is commonly formulated as a probabilistic inference problem over time.

children.name: Semi-supervised Learning
children.short_definition: Semi-supervised learning is a machine learning paradigm that trains models using a combination of labeled and unlabeled data, typically leveraging a small labeled dataset alongside a larger unlabeled one. It aims to improve generalization performance by exploiting the underlying structure of the unlabeled data.

children.name: Self-training
children.short_definition: Self-training is a semi-supervised learning approach in which a model trained on labeled data generates pseudo-labels for unlabeled data and iteratively retrains using selected high-confidence predictions. The process aims to leverage unlabeled data to improve model performance while assuming the initial model’s predictions are sufficiently reliable.

children.short_definition: Accuracy is a performance metric that measures the proportion of correct predictions made by a model out of the total number of predictions. It is commonly used in classification tasks but can be unreliable when class distributions are highly imbalanced.

children.name: Confusion matrix
children.short_definition: A confusion matrix is a tabular representation of a classification model’s performance that cross-tabulates predicted labels against true labels to quantify correct and incorrect predictions. It serves as the basis for deriving evaluation metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score.

children.name: Precision
children.short_definition: Precision is a performance metric in classification tasks that measures the proportion of predicted positive instances that are correctly identified as positive. It quantifies the accuracy of positive predictions by comparing true positives to the total number of positive predictions.

children.name: Recall / Sensitivity
children.short_definition: Recall, also known as sensitivity or true positive rate, is a performance metric that measures the proportion of actual positive instances correctly identified by a model. It is defined as the ratio of true positives to the sum of true positives and false negatives.

children.name: Specificity
children.short_definition: Specificity is an evaluation metric in classification tasks that measures the proportion of actual negative instances correctly identified by a model, defined as the true negative rate equal to the number of true negatives divided by the sum of true negatives and false positives.

children.name: F1 score
children.short_definition: The F1 score is the harmonic mean of precision and recall, providing a single performance metric that balances false positive and false negative errors in classification tasks.

children.name: ROC curve
children.short_definition: A ROC curve, or Receiver Operating Characteristic curve, is a graphical representation of a binary classifier’s performance that plots the true positive rate against the false positive rate across varying decision thresholds. It characterizes the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity and is commonly used to assess discriminatory ability independent of class distribution.

children.name: AUC
children.short_definition: AUC, or Area Under the Curve, is a scalar performance metric that summarizes the area under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve, quantifying a model’s ability to discriminate between classes across all decision thresholds.

children.name: Log loss
children.short_definition: Log loss, also known as logarithmic loss or cross-entropy loss, is a probabilistic loss function that measures the negative log-likelihood of the true labels given predicted class probabilities. It penalizes confident incorrect predictions more heavily and is commonly used to evaluate and train probabilistic classification models.

children.name: Co-training
children.short_definition: Co-training is a semi-supervised learning framework in which multiple classifiers are trained on distinct, conditionally independent feature views of the same data and iteratively improve by labeling unlabeled instances for one another using high-confidence predictions.

children.name: Clustering

children.name: Adjusted Rand Index (ARI)
children.short_definition: The Adjusted Rand Index (ARI) is a measure of similarity between two data clusterings that quantifies agreement while correcting for chance. It ranges from −1 to 1, with higher values indicating greater correspondence between the partitions.

children.name: Normalized Mutual Information (NMI)
children.short_definition: Normalized Mutual Information (NMI) is an information-theoretic measure that quantifies the amount of shared information between two random variables or partitions, normalized by a function of their entropies to control for scale. It produces values typically in the range [0, 1], enabling comparable assessments and invariance to label permutations.

children.name: Silhouette Score
children.short_definition: The Silhouette Score is an internal clustering validation metric that measures how similar a data point is to its own cluster compared to other clusters, based on intra-cluster cohesion and inter-cluster separation. It ranges from −1 to 1, with higher values indicating better-defined and well-separated clusters.

children.name: Label Spreading
children.short_definition: Label Spreading is a graph-based semi-supervised learning algorithm that propagates labels from labeled to unlabeled data points by iteratively diffusing label information across a similarity graph. It assumes that nearby or highly similar samples are likely to share the same label, resulting in smooth label distributions over the data manifold.

It assumes that the decision boundary should pass through low-density regions of the input space to improve generalization.

children.name: Hinge loss
children.short_definition: Hinge loss is a convex loss function used primarily in margin-based classifiers, defined as the maximum of zero and one minus the product of the true label and the predicted score. It penalizes predictions that are incorrect or within the margin, thereby encouraging a large separation between classes.

children.name: Graph-based Semi-supervised Learning
children.short_definition: Graph-based semi-supervised learning is a class of methods that model labeled and unlabeled data as nodes in a graph, with edges representing similarity or relationships, and infer labels by propagating information across the graph structure. It relies on assumptions such as label smoothness over the graph, where connected nodes are likely to share similar labels.

children.name: Node Classification
children.short_definition: Node classification is a supervised or semi-supervised learning task in graph-based machine learning that aims to predict categorical labels for nodes using node attributes and the relational structure of the graph. The objective is to learn from labeled nodes to infer labels for unlabeled nodes based on both feature information and connectivity patterns.

children.name: Unsupervised Learning
children.short_definition: Unsupervised learning is a class of machine learning methods that infer patterns, structure, or representations from unlabeled data without access to explicit target variables. It focuses on modeling underlying data distributions, relationships, or latent factors through self-organizing objectives.

children.name: K-Means Clustering
children.short_definition: K-Means Clustering is an unsupervised learning algorithm that partitions a dataset into a predefined number of clusters by minimizing the within-cluster sum of squared distances between data points and their assigned cluster centroids.

children.name: Clustering
children.short_definition: Clustering is an unsupervised learning process that partitions a set of data points into groups such that points within the same group are more similar to each other than to those in other groups according to a defined similarity or distance measure.

children.name: Silhouette Score
children.short_definition: The Silhouette Score is an internal clustering validation metric that measures how similar a data point is to its own cluster compared to other clusters, based on intra-cluster cohesion and inter-cluster separation. It ranges from −1 to 1, with higher values indicating better-defined and well-separated clusters.

children.name: Davies-Bouldin Index
children.short_definition: The Davies–Bouldin Index is an internal clustering validation metric that quantifies the average similarity between each cluster and its most similar other cluster, based on the ratio of within-cluster dispersion to between-cluster separation. Lower values indicate better clustering with compact, well-separated clusters.

children.name: Calinski-Harabasz Index
children.short_definition: The Calinski-Harabasz Index is an internal cluster validity metric that evaluates clustering quality by computing the ratio of between-cluster dispersion to within-cluster dispersion, adjusted for the number of clusters and data points. Higher values indicate more compact and well-separated clusters.

children.name: Inertia (Within-Cluster SSE)
children.short_definition: Inertia, also known as within-cluster sum of squared errors (SSE), is a clustering metric that quantifies the total squared distance between each data point and the centroid of its assigned cluster. It measures cluster compactness and is commonly minimized by algorithms such as k-means.

children.name: Hierarchical Clustering
children.short_definition: Hierarchical clustering is an unsupervised learning method that organizes data into a tree-like structure of nested clusters based on a measure of similarity or distance. It operates either by iteratively merging smaller clusters into larger ones (agglomerative) or by recursively splitting larger clusters into smaller ones (divisive).

children.name: Clustering

children.name: Cophenetic Correlation Coefficient
children.short_definition: The cophenetic correlation coefficient is a statistical measure used in hierarchical clustering to quantify how accurately a dendrogram preserves the original pairwise distances between data points. It is defined as the correlation between the original distance matrix and the cophenetic distances derived from the dendrogram.

children.name: Silhouette Score

It ranges from −1 to 1, with higher values indicating better-defined and well-separated clusters.

children.name: Adjusted Rand Index (ARI)
children.short_definition: The Adjusted Rand Index (ARI) is a measure of similarity between two data clusterings that quantifies agreement while correcting for chance. It ranges from −1 to 1, with higher values indicating greater correspondence between the partitions.

children.name: DBSCAN
children.short_definition: DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise) is an unsupervised clustering algorithm that groups data points into clusters based on regions of high density while identifying points in low-density regions as noise. It defines clusters using a distance threshold and a minimum number of points, without requiring a predefined number of clusters.

children.name: Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM)
children.short_definition: Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs) are probabilistic models that represent a data distribution as a weighted combination of multiple Gaussian components, each characterized by its own mean and covariance. They are commonly used for density estimation, clustering, and modeling complex, multimodal data distributions.

children.name: Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC)
children.short_definition: The Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC) is a model selection criterion derived from Bayesian inference that approximates the log marginal likelihood while penalizing model complexity as a function of the number of parameters and the sample size. It is commonly expressed as BIC = −2 log L + k log n, where L is the maximized likelihood, k the number of parameters, and n the number of observations.

children.name: Akaike Information Criterion (AIC)
children.short_definition: The Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) is an information-theoretic measure used for statistical model selection that balances model fit against complexity to estimate relative predictive quality. It is defined as AIC = 2k − 2 ln(L), where k is the number of estimated parameters and L is the maximized likelihood, with lower values indicating a preferred model among candidates.

children.name: Silhouette Score
children.short_definition: The Silhouette Score is an internal clustering validation metric that measures how similar a data point is to its own cluster compared to other clusters, based on intra-cluster cohesion and inter-cluster separation. It ranges from −1 to 1, with higher values indicating better-defined and well-separated clusters.

children.name: Density Estimation
children.short_definition: Density estimation is the process of learning an underlying probability distribution from observed data by estimating its probability density function. It can be performed using parametric or nonparametric methods to model how data points are distributed in the input space.

children.name: Log-Likelihood
children.short_definition: Log-likelihood is the natural logarithm of the likelihood function of a statistical model given observed data, typically equivalent to the sum of log-probabilities under independence assumptions. It is widely used for parameter estimation because it simplifies mathematical analysis and improves numerical stability in optimization.

children.name: Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL)
children.short_definition: Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL) is a loss function that measures the negative logarithm of the probability assigned by a statistical model to the observed data under its predicted distribution. It is commonly used for probabilistic modeling and maximum likelihood estimation, where minimizing NLL corresponds to maximizing the likelihood of the data.

children.name: Spectral Clustering
children.short_definition: Spectral clustering is an unsupervised learning method that partitions data by performing eigenvalue decomposition on a similarity or graph Laplacian matrix to embed points into a lower-dimensional space where clusters are more easily separable. Clustering is then performed using standard algorithms on this spectral embedding.
