children.name: Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
children.short_definition: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is a statistical dimensionality reduction technique that transforms high-dimensional data into a lower-dimensional representation by projecting it onto orthogonal directions that maximize variance. It is commonly used to reduce redundancy, improve computational efficiency, and reveal dominant patterns in data.

children.name: Dimensionality Reduction
children.short_definition: Dimensionality reduction is the process of transforming high-dimensional data into a lower-dimensional representation while preserving essential information or structure. It is commonly used to reduce computational complexity, mitigate the effects of high dimensionality, and support efficient analysis and learning.

children.name: Explained Variance Ratio
children.short_definition: Explained Variance Ratio is a statistical measure that quantifies the proportion of the total variance in a dataset that is captured by a particular component, factor, or model. It is commonly used to assess how much information is retained by dimensionality reduction techniques or predictive models relative to the original data.

children.name: Cumulative Explained Variance
children.short_definition: Cumulative explained variance is the proportion of total variance in a dataset accounted for by the first \(k\) components of a dimensionality reduction method, typically computed as the sum of their individual explained variances. It is used to assess how many components are required to capture a desired amount of information in the data.

children.name: Reconstruction Error (MSE)
children.short_definition: Reconstruction error measured by mean squared error (MSE) is a quantitative metric that computes the average of the squared differences between original input data and its reconstructed output from a model. It is commonly used to assess how accurately a model preserves information during encoding and decoding processes.

children.name: Independent Component Analysis (ICA)
children.short_definition: Independent Component Analysis (ICA) is a statistical signal processing technique that decomposes multivariate data into components that are maximally statistically independent, assuming the observed data are linear mixtures of non-Gaussian source signals. It is commonly used for blind source separation and unsupervised feature extraction.

children.name: Dimensionality Reduction
children.short_definition: Dimensionality reduction is the process of transforming high-dimensional data into a lower-dimensional representation while preserving essential information or structure. It is commonly used to reduce computational complexity, mitigate the effects of high dimensionality, and support efficient analysis and learning.

children.name: Reconstruction Error (MSE)
children.short_definition: Reconstruction error measured by mean squared error (MSE) is a quantitative metric that computes the average of the squared differences between original input data and its reconstructed output from a model. It is commonly used to assess how accurately a model preserves information during encoding and decoding processes.

children.name: Kurtosis
children.short_definition: Kurtosis is a statistical measure that quantifies the shape of a probability distribution by describing the weight of its tails relative to the overall distribution. It indicates the propensity of a dataset to produce extreme values, reflecting tail heaviness. Note that kurtosis primarily measures tail weight rather than peak sharpness or flatness.

children.name: t-SNE
children.short_definition: t-SNE (t-distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding) is a nonlinear dimensionality reduction technique used primarily for visualizing high-dimensional data in low-dimensional spaces. It models pairwise similarities with probability distributions and minimizes the Kullback–Leibler divergence between high-dimensional similarities and a low-dimensional embedding using a heavy-tailed Student’s t-distribution.

children.name: Dimensionality Reduction
children.short_definition: Dimensionality reduction is the process of transforming high-dimensional data into a lower-dimensional representation while preserving essential information or structure. It is commonly used to reduce computational complexity, mitigate the effects of high dimensionality, and support efficient analysis and learning.

children.name: Kullback-Leibler Divergence
children.short_definition: Kullback–Leibler divergence is a measure from information theory that quantifies the difference between two probability distributions by measuring the expected logarithmic discrepancy between them. It is asymmetric and represents the information loss incurred when one distribution is used to approximate another.

children.name: Trustworthiness
children.short_definition: Trustworthiness is the degree to which an artificial intelligence system can be relied upon to operate as intended, in a consistent and predictable manner, under defined conditions. It reflects the extent to which the system’s design, implementation, and operation provide justified confidence in its behavior throughout its lifecycle.

children.name: UMAP
children.short_definition: UMAP (Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection) is a nonlinear dimensionality reduction algorithm that constructs a low-dimensional representation of high-dimensional data by preserving local neighborhood structure based on manifold learning and topological principles. It is commonly used for visualization and representation learning in high-dimensional datasets.

children.name: Dimensionality Reduction
children.short_definition: Dimensionality reduction is the process of transforming high-dimensional data into a lower-dimensional representation while preserving essential information or structure. It is commonly used to reduce computational complexity, mitigate the effects of high dimensionality, and support efficient analysis and learning.

children.name: Trustworthiness
children.short_definition: Trustworthiness is the degree to which an artificial intelligence system can be relied upon to operate as intended, in a consistent and predictable manner, under defined conditions. It reflects the extent to which the system’s design, implementation, and operation provide justified confidence in its behavior throughout its lifecycle.

children.name: Continuity
children.short_definition: Continuity is a property of a function or process whereby arbitrarily small changes in input result in arbitrarily small changes in output, as formally defined in topology or real analysis. In computational and AI contexts, it often characterizes smoothness or stability of mappings and state transitions.

children.name: Isolation Forest
children.short_definition: Isolation Forest is an unsupervised anomaly detection algorithm that identifies outliers by recursively partitioning data using randomly selected features and split values, isolating anomalous points with fewer splits. It is based on the principle that anomalies are more susceptible to isolation than normal observations.

children.name: Anomaly Detection
children.short_definition: Anomaly detection is the task of identifying data points, sequences, or patterns that deviate significantly from an expected model or distribution of normal behavior. It employs statistical, machine learning, or signal processing methods to characterize normality and detect deviations.

children.name: Precision
children.short_definition: Precision is a performance metric in classification tasks that measures the proportion of predicted positive instances that are correctly identified as positive. It quantifies the accuracy of positive predictions by comparing true positives to the total number of positive predictions.

children.name: Recall
children.short_definition: Recall is a performance metric in classification that measures the proportion of actual positive instances correctly identified by a model, calculated as the ratio of true positives to the sum of true positives and false negatives. It reflects a model’s ability to capture all relevant instances of a target class.

children.name: F1 score
children.short_definition: The F1 score is the harmonic mean of precision and recall, providing a single performance metric that balances false positive and false negative errors in classification tasks.

children.name: ROC curve
children.short_definition: A ROC curve, or Receiver Operating Characteristic curve, is a graphical representation of a binary classifier’s performance that plots the true positive rate against the false positive rate across varying decision thresholds. It characterizes the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity and is commonly used to assess discriminatory ability independent of class distribution.

children.name: AUC
children.short_definition: AUC, or Area Under the Curve, is a scalar performance metric that summarizes the area under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve, quantifying a model’s ability to discriminate between classes across all decision thresholds.

children.name: Association Rule Mining (Apriori / FP-Growth)
children.short_definition: Association rule mining is a data mining task that discovers frequent itemsets and infers associative relationships among items in large datasets based on measures such as support and confidence. Apriori and FP-Growth are core algorithms for this task, with Apriori using iterative candidate generation and pruning based on the downward closure property, and FP-Growth employing a compressed tree structure to mine frequent patterns without explicit candidate generation.

children.name: Association Analysis
children.short_definition: Association analysis is a data mining technique that identifies statistically significant relationships, co-occurrence patterns, or dependencies among variables within large datasets. It is commonly used to discover association rules that describe how items or events are related based on measures such as support, confidence, and lift.

children.name: Support
children.short_definition: Support refers to the organized provision of assistance for the use, maintenance, and troubleshooting of a system or technology, encompassing guidance, problem resolution, and operational help. In computing and AI contexts, support is typically delivered through online resources and services that facilitate effective system adoption and continued use.

children.name: Confidence
children.short_definition: Confidence is a measure of the degree of certainty associated with a model’s prediction or inferred belief, typically represented as a probability or scalar score. It reflects the model’s internal assessment of how strongly the available information supports a particular outcome.

children.name: Lift
children.short_definition: In machine learning and data mining, lift is a statistical measure that quantifies the strength of an association between variables by comparing their observed co-occurrence to the expected co-occurrence under independence. It is commonly used to evaluate association rules and model performance relative to a baseline probability.

children.name: Conviction
children.short_definition: Conviction is the degree of belief or certainty that an intelligent agent assigns to a proposition, hypothesis, or decision. It reflects the agent’s internal assessment of evidential support, often represented quantitatively as confidence or probability.

children.name: Reinforcement Learning
children.short_definition: Reinforcement Learning is a machine learning paradigm in which an agent learns a policy by interacting with an environment and optimizing actions to maximize expected cumulative reward. It is commonly formalized using sequential decision-making frameworks such as Markov decision processes.

children.name: Multi-Armed Bandits
children.short_definition: Multi-armed bandits are a class of sequential decision-making problems in which an agent repeatedly chooses among multiple actions with unknown reward distributions, balancing exploration and exploitation to maximize cumulative reward. They are commonly modeled using stochastic processes and studied within reinforcement learning and online learning theory.

children.name: Action Selection / Optimization
children.short_definition: Action selection and optimization is the decision-making process by which an intelligent system evaluates possible actions and selects those that maximize or minimize a defined objective function under given constraints and uncertainty. It is typically formalized through policies, value functions, or optimization algorithms that map states or observations to optimal actions.

children.name: Cumulative Reward
children.short_definition: Cumulative reward is the total reward accumulated by an agent over a sequence of time steps in a reinforcement learning process, typically defined as the sum of received rewards. It may be computed with a discount factor to weight immediate rewards more heavily than future rewards.

children.name: Average Reward
children.short_definition: Average reward is a reinforcement learning objective that measures the long-run expected reward per time step obtained by an agent under a given policy, typically defined as the limit of the expected cumulative reward divided by time as the horizon grows. It is used to evaluate steady-state performance in continuing tasks without discounting.

children.name: Regret
children.short_definition: Regret is a performance measure in online learning and sequential decision-making defined as the cumulative difference between the loss (or reward) incurred by an algorithm and that of an optimal strategy chosen in hindsight over the same sequence of decisions.

children.name: Simple Regret
children.short_definition: Simple regret is a performance measure in sequential decision-making that quantifies the expected difference between the optimal achievable reward and the reward of the single action recommended after a learning process. It evaluates the quality of the final decision rather than the cumulative rewards obtained during learning.

children.name: Dynamic Programming (RL)
children.short_definition: Dynamic Programming in Reinforcement Learning refers to a class of methods that compute optimal value functions and policies by recursively applying Bellman equations over a known Markov Decision Process model. These methods rely on full knowledge of state transition dynamics and rewards and typically involve iterative policy evaluation and policy improvement.

children.name: Policy Evaluation
children.short_definition: Policy evaluation is the process in reinforcement learning of computing the expected return or value function for a given policy, based on the dynamics of the environment and the reward function. It assesses how well a fixed policy performs without modifying the policy itself.

children.name: Mean Squared Value Error (MSVE)
children.short_definition: Mean Squared Value Error (MSVE) is a quantitative metric used in reinforcement learning to measure the average squared difference between an estimated value function and the true value function over a given state distribution. It evaluates the accuracy of value function approximation by capturing both bias and variance in the estimates.

children.name: Bellman Error
children.short_definition: Bellman error is the discrepancy between the two sides of the Bellman equation, measuring how well a value function satisfies the recursive relationship defined by expected immediate rewards and discounted future values. It is commonly used as an objective or diagnostic quantity in reinforcement learning to assess and optimize value function approximations.

children.name: Policy Optimization
children.short_definition: Policy optimization is a class of methods in reinforcement learning that aim to directly improve a parameterized policy by optimizing an objective function representing expected cumulative reward. These methods typically use gradient-based techniques to adjust policy parameters based on performance feedback from interactions with an environment.

children.name: Expected Return
children.short_definition: Expected return is the expected cumulative reward obtained by an agent, defined as the mathematical expectation of the sum of future rewards, often discounted over time. It serves as the primary objective function optimized in reinforcement learning and decision-theoretic models.

children.name: Policy Loss
children.short_definition: Policy loss is an objective function used in reinforcement learning to quantify how well a policy’s action distribution aligns with desired behavior, typically by measuring the discrepancy between predicted actions and targets derived from rewards or advantage estimates. It guides policy optimization by providing gradients for updating policy parameters during training.

children.name: Monte Carlo Methods
children.short_definition: Monte Carlo methods are a class of stochastic computational algorithms that use repeated random sampling to approximate numerical quantities. They are widely used for estimation, integration, optimization, and uncertainty analysis in problems where deterministic solutions are intractable.

children.name: Policy Evaluation

It assesses how well a fixed policy performs without modifying the policy itself.

children.name: Variance of Returns
children.short_definition: Variance of returns is a statistical measure that quantifies the dispersion of returns around their expected value, defined as the expected squared deviation from the mean return. It is commonly used to characterize the risk or uncertainty associated with an investment or stochastic process.

children.name: Temporal-Difference Learning (TD)
children.short_definition: Temporal-Difference Learning (TD) is a class of reinforcement learning methods that update value function estimates by bootstrapping from subsequent predictions, using the difference between successive predictions as a learning signal. It combines aspects of dynamic programming and Monte Carlo methods by learning directly from raw experience without requiring a model of the environment or complete episodes.

children.name: Value Function Estimation
children.short_definition: Value function estimation is the process of approximating a function that assigns an expected cumulative return to each state or state–action pair under a given policy in a decision-making problem. It is a core component of reinforcement learning, enabling evaluation and improvement of policies when the true value function is unknown.

children.name: TD Error
children.short_definition: TD error (temporal-difference error) is the difference between a value estimate and a bootstrap target composed of the immediate reward plus the discounted estimate of the next state’s value, and it quantifies the discrepancy used to drive learning updates in temporal-difference reinforcement learning.

children.name: Mean Squared TD Error
children.short_definition: Mean Squared TD Error is a loss function in reinforcement learning that measures the expected squared difference between the predicted value function and the temporal-difference target. It quantifies the discrepancy between current value estimates and bootstrapped estimates based on observed rewards and subsequent state values.

children.name: Q-Learning
children.short_definition: Q-Learning is a model-free reinforcement learning algorithm that learns an optimal action–value function by iteratively updating estimates of expected cumulative reward using temporal-difference learning. It enables an agent to derive an optimal policy by selecting actions that maximize the learned Q-values without requiring a model of the environment.

children.name: Action-Value Estimation
children.short_definition: Action-value estimation is the process in reinforcement learning of estimating the expected cumulative return of taking a specific action in a given state and subsequently following a particular policy. It typically involves approximating the action-value function, which quantifies long-term rewards associated with state–action pairs.

It converges toward the value of the policy being followed, incorporating the effects of exploration into learning.

children.name: Policy Gradient Methods
children.short_definition: Policy gradient methods are reinforcement learning algorithms that optimize a parameterized policy by directly estimating and ascending the gradient of the expected cumulative reward with respect to the policy parameters. They operate in policy space, often using stochastic policies and gradient estimators derived from the policy gradient theorem.

children.name: Entropy
children.short_definition: Entropy is a quantitative measure of uncertainty or randomness in a probability distribution, commonly defined in information theory as the expected value of the information content of a random variable. It characterizes the amount of unpredictability or disorder present in a system or data source.

children.name: Actor-Critic Methods
children.short_definition: Actor-critic methods are a class of reinforcement learning algorithms that combine policy-based and value-based approaches by maintaining separate structures for the policy (actor) and the value function (critic). The actor updates the policy based on feedback from the critic, which evaluates actions using estimated value functions.

children.name: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning
children.short_definition: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning is a reinforcement learning paradigm in which the agent learns or is given an explicit model of the environment’s dynamics and uses this model to plan, predict future states and rewards, or improve policy learning. It contrasts with model-free approaches by leveraging environment models to increase sample efficiency and enable planning-based decision making.

children.name: Dynamics Modeling
children.short_definition: Dynamics modeling is the construction of mathematical or computational representations that describe how a system’s state evolves over time according to deterministic or stochastic laws. It formalizes state transitions and temporal dependencies to enable analysis, prediction, and control of dynamic systems.

children.name: Prediction Error (MSE)
children.short_definition: Prediction Error (MSE), or Mean Squared Error, is a loss metric defined as the average of the squared differences between predicted values and corresponding ground-truth values. It measures prediction accuracy by disproportionately penalizing larger deviations and is widely used in regression and forecasting model evaluation.

children.name: Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL)
children.short_definition: Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL) is a loss function that measures the negative logarithm of the probability assigned by a statistical model to the observed data under its predicted distribution. It is commonly used for probabilistic modeling and maximum likelihood estimation, where minimizing NLL corresponds to maximizing the likelihood of the data.

children.name: Planning
children.short_definition: Planning is an area of artificial intelligence concerned with the automated generation of action sequences that transform an initial state into a desired goal state based on a model of the environment. It typically involves reasoning about states, actions, constraints, and their effects to select actions that achieve objectives.

children.name: Expected Return

It serves as the primary objective function optimized in reinforcement learning and decision-theoretic models.

children.name: Cumulative Reward
children.short_definition: Cumulative reward is the total reward accumulated by an agent over a sequence of time steps in a reinforcement learning process, typically defined as the sum of received rewards. It may be computed with a discount factor to weight immediate rewards more heavily than future rewards.

children.name: Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL)
children.short_definition: Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) is a framework in which the reward function underlying an observed agent’s behavior is inferred from demonstrations, assuming the agent acts approximately optimally with respect to that reward. It is commonly used to model preferences or objectives when the reward function is not explicitly specified.

children.name: Reward Function Learning
children.short_definition: Reward function learning is the process of inferring or optimizing a reward function that specifies desired objectives or preferences, typically from data such as demonstrations, feedback, or observed behavior. It is commonly studied in reinforcement learning and inverse reinforcement learning as a means to align agent behavior with intended goals.

It is commonly used for probabilistic modeling and maximum likelihood estimation, where minimizing NLL corresponds to maximizing the likelihood of the data.

children.name: Feature Expectation Error
children.short_definition: Feature expectation error is a quantitative measure of the discrepancy between the expected feature counts induced by a learned policy or model and the target feature expectations, commonly those derived from expert behavior. It is typically defined as a norm of the difference between these feature expectation vectors and is used to assess learning accuracy in inverse reinforcement learning.

children.name: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL)
children.short_definition: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) is a subfield of reinforcement learning that studies how multiple learning agents interact within a shared environment, where each agent’s actions influence the dynamics and rewards of others. It focuses on learning policies under conditions of cooperation, competition, or mixed incentives, often involving non-stationary environments from the perspective of individual agents.

children.name: Cooperative Learning
children.short_definition: Cooperative learning is a machine learning paradigm in which multiple agents or models learn jointly by sharing information or coordinating their actions to optimize individual or collective objectives. It is commonly studied in multi-agent reinforcement learning and distributed learning systems, where agents may cooperate through communication, shared experiences, or coordinated policies to improve learning efficiency or achieve common goals.

children.name: Joint Cumulative Reward
children.short_definition: Joint cumulative reward is the total reward accumulated over time by a group of agents according to a joint reward function that depends on their collective states and actions. It serves as an objective measure for evaluating or optimizing coordinated behavior in multi-agent reinforcement learning.

children.name: Average Team Reward
children.short_definition: Average Team Reward is a performance metric in multi-agent reinforcement learning defined as the arithmetic mean of the rewards received by all agents over a specified time horizon or episode, representing the collective outcome of the team’s behavior.

children.name: Competitive Learning
children.short_definition: Competitive learning is an unsupervised learning paradigm in which multiple units or models compete to respond to input data, and only the best-matching unit is updated based on the input. It is commonly used to discover structure in data by encouraging specialization among units through competition.

children.name: Win Rate
children.short_definition: Win rate is a performance metric that measures the proportion of successful outcomes relative to the total number of trials, episodes, or matches in a given evaluation setting. It is commonly used to assess the effectiveness of algorithms, agents, or strategies in competitive or decision-making tasks.

children.name: Exploitability
children.short_definition: Exploitability is the degree to which a system, algorithm, or policy can be taken advantage of by an adversary, often quantified as the performance gap between the system and an optimal best response in adversarial or game-theoretic settings.

children.name: Deep Learning
children.short_definition: Deep learning is a subfield of machine learning that uses multilayer artificial neural networks to automatically learn hierarchical representations from data. It enables end-to-end modeling of complex patterns through gradient-based optimization of network parameters.

children.name: Deep Feedforward Networks
children.short_definition: Deep feedforward networks are neural network architectures composed of multiple stacked layers in which information flows unidirectionally from input to output without cycles or feedback connections. They are typically trained using gradient-based optimization and backpropagation to learn hierarchical representations for function approximation or prediction tasks.

children.name: Classification
children.short_definition: Classification is a supervised learning task in which a model assigns input instances to one of a finite set of predefined categories based on their features. It involves learning decision boundaries or probabilistic mappings from labeled training data.

children.name: Accuracy
children.short_definition: Accuracy is a performance metric that measures the proportion of correct predictions made by a model out of the total number of predictions. It is commonly used in classification tasks but can be unreliable when class distributions are highly imbalanced.

children.name: Confusion matrix
children.short_definition: A confusion matrix is a tabular representation of a classification model’s performance that cross-tabulates predicted labels against true labels to quantify correct and incorrect predictions. It serves as the basis for deriving evaluation metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score.

children.name: Precision
children.short_definition: Precision is a performance metric in classification tasks that measures the proportion of predicted positive instances that are correctly identified as positive. It quantifies the accuracy of positive predictions by comparing true positives to the total number of positive predictions.

children.name: Recall / Sensitivity
children.short_definition: Recall, also known as sensitivity or true positive rate, is a performance metric that measures the proportion of actual positive instances correctly identified by a model. It is defined as the ratio of true positives to the sum of true positives and false negatives.

children.name: Specificity
children.short_definition: Specificity is an evaluation metric in classification tasks that measures the proportion of actual negative instances correctly identified by a model, defined as the true negative rate equal to the number of true negatives divided by the sum of true negatives and false positives.

children.name: F1 score
children.short_definition: The F1 score is the harmonic mean of precision and recall, providing a single performance metric that balances false positive and false negative errors in classification tasks.

children.name: ROC curve
children.short_definition: A ROC curve, or Receiver Operating Characteristic curve, is a graphical representation of a binary classifier’s performance that plots the true positive rate against the false positive rate across varying decision thresholds. It characterizes the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity and is commonly used to assess discriminatory ability independent of class distribution.

children.name: AUC
children.short_definition: AUC, or Area Under the Curve, is a scalar performance metric that summarizes the area under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve, quantifying a model’s ability to discriminate between classes across all decision thresholds.

children.name: Log loss
children.short_definition: Log loss, also known as logarithmic loss or cross-entropy loss, is a probabilistic loss function that measures the negative log-likelihood of the true labels given predicted class probabilities. It penalizes confident incorrect predictions more heavily and is commonly used to evaluate and train probabilistic classification models.

children.name: Regression
children.short_definition: Regression is a supervised learning paradigm that models the relationship between one or more independent variables and a continuous dependent variable by estimating a function that minimizes prediction error. It is commonly used for prediction, forecasting, and quantifying variable relationships.

children.name: Mean Squared Error (MSE)
children.short_definition: Mean Squared Error (MSE) is a loss function that measures the average of the squared differences between predicted values and corresponding true values in a dataset. It quantifies prediction error magnitude by penalizing larger errors more heavily due to squaring.

children.name: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)
children.short_definition: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) is a metric that quantifies the average magnitude of prediction errors by taking the square root of the mean of squared differences between predicted and observed values. It is expressed in the same units as the target variable and is sensitive to large errors.

children.name: Mean Absolute Error (MAE)
children.short_definition: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) is a regression evaluation metric that measures the average of the absolute differences between predicted values and true values. It quantifies prediction accuracy by expressing errors in the same units as the target variable.

children.name: R² (Coefficient of Determination)
children.short_definition: The coefficient of determination (R²) is a statistical measure that quantifies the proportion of variance in a dependent variable that is explained by an independent variable or model. It provides an indication of model fit by assessing how well observed outcomes are replicated by the model’s predictions.

children.name: Convolutional Neural Networks (Deep CNN)
children.short_definition: Convolutional Neural Networks (Deep CNNs) are a class of deep neural networks that use stacked convolutional layers with shared weights and local receptive fields to automatically learn hierarchical feature representations from structured data, particularly grid-like inputs. They are commonly used in tasks involving visual, spatial, or temporal pattern recognition.

children.name: Image Classification
children.short_definition: Image classification is a computer vision task that assigns one or more predefined labels to an image based on its visual content. It is typically performed using machine learning or deep learning models that learn discriminative features from labeled image data.

children.name: Accuracy
children.short_definition: Accuracy is a performance metric that measures the proportion of correct predictions made by a model out of the total number of predictions. It is commonly used in classification tasks but can be unreliable when class distributions are highly imbalanced.

children.name: Top-k Accuracy
children.short_definition: Top-k accuracy is an evaluation metric for classification models that measures the proportion of instances in which the true label appears among the model’s k highest-scoring predicted classes. It is commonly used when multiple plausible predictions are acceptable or when class ambiguity is present.

children.name: Precision
children.short_definition: Precision is a performance metric in classification tasks that measures the proportion of predicted positive instances that are correctly identified as positive. It quantifies the accuracy of positive predictions by comparing true positives to the total number of positive predictions.

children.name: Recall
children.short_definition: Recall is a performance metric in classification that measures the proportion of actual positive instances correctly identified by a model, calculated as the ratio of true positives to the sum of true positives and false negatives. It reflects a model’s ability to capture all relevant instances of a target class.

children.name: F1 score
children.short_definition: The F1 score is the harmonic mean of precision and recall, providing a single performance metric that balances false positive and false negative errors in classification tasks.

children.name: Object Detection
children.short_definition: Object detection is a computer vision task that involves identifying and localizing instances of predefined object classes within an image or video, typically by predicting bounding boxes and associated class labels. It combines elements of image classification and spatial localization to determine both what objects are present and where they appear.

children.name: Intersection over Union (IoU)
children.short_definition: Intersection over Union (IoU) is a similarity metric that quantifies the overlap between two spatial regions by computing the ratio of the area of their intersection to the area of their union. It is commonly used to evaluate the accuracy of predicted regions against reference regions in computer vision tasks.

children.name: mean Average Precision (mAP)
children.short_definition: Mean Average Precision (mAP) is an evaluation metric for information retrieval and detection systems that computes the mean of average precision scores across multiple queries, classes, or categories. It summarizes both precision and recall by integrating precision over recall levels, providing a single scalar measure of ranking or detection performance.

children.name: Precision

It quantifies the accuracy of positive predictions by comparing true positives to the total number of positive predictions.

children.name: Recall

It reflects a model’s ability to capture all relevant instances of a target class.

children.name: Image Segmentation
children.short_definition: Image segmentation is the process of partitioning a digital image into multiple disjoint regions or segments such that pixels within each region share similar visual or semantic characteristics. It is commonly used to simplify image representation and to enable object-level analysis in computer vision tasks.

children.name: Dice Coefficient
children.short_definition: The Dice coefficient, also known as the Sørensen–Dice coefficient, is a statistical similarity measure that quantifies the overlap between two sets as twice the size of their intersection divided by the sum of their sizes. It is commonly used to evaluate agreement between binary data, particularly in image segmentation and information retrieval.

children.name: Jaccard Index (IoU)
children.short_definition: The Jaccard Index, also known as Intersection over Union (IoU), is a similarity metric defined as the ratio of the size of the intersection of two sets to the size of their union. It is commonly used to quantify overlap between predicted and ground truth regions in tasks such as image segmentation and object detection.

children.name: Pixel Accuracy
children.short_definition: Pixel accuracy is an evaluation metric for image segmentation and classification tasks that measures the proportion of pixels whose predicted labels exactly match the ground truth labels across an image or dataset. It provides a simple overall measure of classification correctness at the pixel level.

children.name: Recurrent Neural Networks (Deep RNN)
children.short_definition: Deep Recurrent Neural Networks (Deep RNNs) are neural network architectures composed of multiple stacked recurrent layers that process sequential data by maintaining and updating hidden states over time. They enable hierarchical temporal feature learning and are used to model complex temporal dependencies in sequences.

children.name: Sequence Classification
children.short_definition: Sequence classification is a supervised learning task that assigns a discrete label to an entire ordered sequence by modeling dependencies and structure across its elements.

children.name: Time Series Forecasting
children.short_definition: Time series forecasting is the task of modeling and predicting future values of a temporally ordered sequence based on its historical patterns, trends, and temporal dependencies. It typically involves statistical or machine learning methods that account for time-dependent structure such as seasonality, autocorrelation, and non-stationarity.

children.name: Mean Absolute Error (MAE)
children.short_definition: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) is a regression evaluation metric that measures the average of the absolute differences between predicted values and true values. It quantifies prediction accuracy by expressing errors in the same units as the target variable.

children.name: MAPE / SMAPE
children.short_definition: MAPE (Mean Absolute Percentage Error) is an evaluation metric that measures the average absolute difference between predicted and actual values expressed as a percentage of the actual values. SMAPE (Symmetric Mean Absolute Percentage Error) is a related metric that normalizes the absolute error by the average magnitude of the predicted and actual values, providing a bounded and more symmetric measure of relative prediction error.

children.name: Transformers
children.short_definition: Transformers are a class of neural network architectures designed for sequence modeling that rely on self-attention mechanisms rather than recurrence or convolution to capture dependencies among elements in an input sequence. They enable parallel computation and scalable learning of long-range relationships and form the foundation of many modern natural language processing and multimodal models.

children.name: Sequence Classification
children.short_definition: Sequence classification is a supervised learning task that assigns a discrete label to an entire ordered sequence by modeling dependencies and structure across its elements.

children.name: Accuracy

It reflects a model’s ability to capture all relevant instances of a target class.

children.name: F1 score
children.short_definition: The F1 score is the harmonic mean of precision and recall, providing a single performance metric that balances false positive and false negative errors in classification tasks.

children.name: ROC curve
children.short_definition: A ROC curve, or Receiver Operating Characteristic curve, is a graphical representation of a binary classifier’s performance that plots the true positive rate against the false positive rate across varying decision thresholds. It characterizes the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity and is commonly used to assess discriminatory ability independent of class distribution.

children.name: AUC
children.short_definition: AUC, or Area Under the Curve, is a scalar performance metric that summarizes the area under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve, quantifying a model’s ability to discriminate between classes across all decision thresholds.

children.name: Sequence-to-Sequence
children.short_definition: Sequence-to-sequence is a neural network modeling framework that maps an input sequence to an output sequence, potentially of different length, by learning conditional dependencies between sequential elements. It is commonly implemented using encoder–decoder architectures that process variable-length data in tasks such as language and time-series modeling.

children.name: BLEU
children.short_definition: BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy) is an automatic evaluation metric for machine translation that measures n-gram precision of a candidate translation against one or more reference translations with a brevity penalty. It produces a corpus-level score intended to correlate with human judgments of translation quality.

children.name: ROUGE
children.short_definition: ROUGE (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation) is a set of automatic evaluation metrics for natural language generation tasks that measure the overlap between generated text and reference texts based on n-grams, sequences, or structural units. It is commonly used to assess the quality of summarization and text generation systems by quantifying content similarity.

children.name: METEOR
children.short_definition: METEOR is an automatic evaluation metric for machine translation that measures similarity between a machine-generated translation and reference translations using exact, stem, synonym, and paraphrase matches. It incorporates precision, recall, and fragmentation penalties to better correlate with human judgment of translation quality.

children.name: Perplexity
children.short_definition: Perplexity is a metric for evaluating probabilistic models, particularly language models, defined as the exponential of the average negative log-likelihood (cross-entropy) of a dataset under the model. Lower perplexity indicates greater predictive confidence and better model fit to the data.

children.name: Language Modeling
children.short_definition: Language modeling is the task of learning a probabilistic representation of natural language that estimates the likelihood of sequences of tokens based on their context. It underlies many natural language processing systems by enabling prediction, generation, and evaluation of linguistic sequences.

children.name: Perplexity
children.short_definition: Perplexity is a metric for evaluating probabilistic models, particularly language models, defined as the exponential of the average negative log-likelihood (cross-entropy) of a dataset under the model. Lower perplexity indicates greater predictive confidence and better model fit to the data.

children.name: Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL)
children.short_definition: Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL) is a loss function that measures the negative logarithm of the probability assigned by a statistical model to the observed data under its predicted distribution. It is commonly used for probabilistic modeling and maximum likelihood estimation, where minimizing NLL corresponds to maximizing the likelihood of the data.

children.name: Autoencoders (Deep)
children.short_definition: Deep autoencoders are neural network architectures composed of multiple stacked layers that learn to encode input data into a compact latent representation and decode it to reconstruct the original input. They are typically trained in an unsupervised or self-supervised manner to learn hierarchical feature representations by minimizing reconstruction error.

children.name: Dimensionality Reduction
children.short_definition: Dimensionality reduction is the process of transforming high-dimensional data into a lower-dimensional representation while preserving essential information or structure. It is commonly used to reduce computational complexity, mitigate the effects of high dimensionality, and support efficient analysis and learning.

children.name: Reconstruction Error (MSE)
children.short_definition: Reconstruction error measured by mean squared error (MSE) is a quantitative metric that computes the average of the squared differences between original input data and its reconstructed output from a model. It is commonly used to assess how accurately a model preserves information during encoding and decoding processes.

children.name: Reconstruction Error (MAE)
children.short_definition: Reconstruction Error (MAE) is a metric that quantifies the average absolute difference between an original input and its reconstructed version produced by a model. It measures reconstruction fidelity by computing the mean of element-wise absolute errors across all input dimensions.

children.name: Anomaly Detection
children.short_definition: Anomaly detection is the task of identifying data points, sequences, or patterns that deviate significantly from an expected model or distribution of normal behavior. It employs statistical, machine learning, or signal processing methods to characterize normality and detect deviations.

children.name: Precision

It quantifies the accuracy of positive predictions by comparing true positives to the total number of positive predictions.

children.name: Recall

It reflects a model’s ability to capture all relevant instances of a target class.

It characterizes the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity and is commonly used to assess discriminatory ability independent of class distribution.

children.name: AUC

children.name: Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)
children.short_definition: Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) are a class of deep learning models consisting of two neural networks, a generator and a discriminator, trained simultaneously in an adversarial process where the generator learns to produce data resembling a target distribution while the discriminator learns to distinguish generated data from real data. This competitive training framework enables the generation of high-fidelity synthetic data across various domains.

children.name: Image Generation
children.short_definition: Image generation is the task of synthesizing visual images by learning and sampling from data distributions using computational models. It may be performed unconditionally or conditioned on auxiliary inputs such as textual descriptions, labels, or other visual signals.

children.name: Inception Score (IS)
children.short_definition: The Inception Score (IS) is a quantitative metric for evaluating generative image models that measures both the quality and diversity of generated images using the output probability distribution of a pretrained Inception network. It is computed based on the KL divergence between the conditional label distribution for each image and the marginal label distribution across all generated images.

children.name: Fréchet Inception Distance (FID)
children.short_definition: Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) is a quantitative metric for evaluating generative models by measuring the distance between the multivariate Gaussian distributions of feature embeddings extracted from real and generated data using a pretrained Inception network. It computes the Fréchet (Wasserstein-2) distance between these distributions, with smaller values indicating greater similarity.

children.name: Data Augmentation
children.short_definition: Data augmentation is a set of techniques that increase the size and diversity of a training dataset by applying systematic transformations or modifications to existing data while preserving its underlying labels or semantics. It is commonly used in machine learning to improve model generalization and reduce overfitting.

children.name: Downstream Task Accuracy
children.short_definition: Downstream task accuracy is a performance metric that measures how well a trained model performs on a specific application task that relies on its learned representations or outputs. It quantifies the proportion of correct predictions or decisions made when the model is applied to a target task distinct from its original training objective.

children.name: Feature Representation Quality
children.short_definition: Feature representation quality refers to the degree to which a set of features accurately, efficiently, and robustly encodes the underlying information in data that is relevant to a learning task. High-quality representations facilitate effective learning, discrimination, and generalization by preserving task-relevant structure while minimizing noise and redundancy.

children.name: Transfer Learning Effectiveness
children.short_definition: Transfer Learning Effectiveness is the extent to which knowledge transferred from a source model or task improves performance, learning efficiency, or generalization on a target task compared to training without transfer. It reflects how well transferred representations align with the target data distribution and objectives.

children.name: Task-Specific Fine-Tuning
children.short_definition: Task-specific fine-tuning is the process of adapting a pre-trained machine learning model to a particular task by further training it on task-relevant data, typically with a smaller learning rate. This procedure adjusts the model’s parameters to optimize performance for a narrowly defined objective while retaining general knowledge from pre-training.

children.name: Domain Adaptation
children.short_definition: Domain adaptation is a machine learning paradigm in which a model trained on a source domain is adapted to perform effectively on a target domain with a different data distribution. It addresses distribution shift by leveraging shared structure or knowledge between domains, often with limited or no labeled data in the target domain.

children.name: Evaluation Metrics Selection
children.short_definition: Evaluation Metrics Selection is the process of choosing appropriate quantitative measures to assess machine learning model performance in alignment with task objectives, data properties, and decision requirements. It ensures that model evaluation and optimization reflect relevant performance trade-offs and operational constraints.

children.name: Label Noise
children.short_definition: Label noise refers to inaccuracies, inconsistencies, or uncertainty in the target labels of a dataset used for supervised learning, often arising from annotation errors, ambiguity, or stochastic labeling processes. It degrades the quality of the learning signal and can adversely affect model training, generalization, and evaluation.

children.name: Class Imbalance
children.short_definition: Class imbalance is a property of a dataset in which the distribution of instances across target classes is highly unequal. It can bias learning algorithms toward majority classes, adversely affecting predictive performance on minority classes.

children.name: Hyperparameter Optimization
children.short_definition: Hyperparameter optimization is the process of systematically selecting values for model hyperparameters to improve learning performance according to a defined evaluation criterion. It operates outside the training procedure and typically involves search strategies over a predefined hyperparameter space.

children.name: Diffusion Models
children.short_definition: Diffusion models are a class of generative probabilistic models that learn to generate data by reversing a gradual stochastic noising process through a sequence of denoising steps. They are typically trained using variants of denoising score matching to approximate the data distribution.

children.name: Image Generation
children.short_definition: Image generation is the task of synthesizing visual images by learning and sampling from data distributions using computational models. It may be performed unconditionally or conditioned on auxiliary inputs such as textual descriptions, labels, or other visual signals.

It computes the Fréchet (Wasserstein-2) distance between these distributions, with smaller values indicating greater similarity.

It is computed based on the KL divergence between the conditional label distribution for each image and the marginal label distribution across all generated images.

children.name: Deep Reinforcement Learning
children.short_definition: Deep reinforcement learning is a class of methods that integrate reinforcement learning with deep neural networks to learn policies or value functions from high-dimensional sensory inputs through interaction with an environment. It optimizes behavior by maximizing expected cumulative reward using gradient-based learning and trial-and-error feedback.

children.name: Policy Optimization
children.short_definition: Policy optimization is a class of methods in reinforcement learning that aim to directly improve a parameterized policy by optimizing an objective function representing expected cumulative reward. These methods typically use gradient-based techniques to adjust policy parameters based on performance feedback from interactions with an environment.

children.name: Cumulative Reward
children.short_definition: Cumulative reward is the total reward accumulated by an agent over a sequence of time steps in a reinforcement learning process, typically defined as the sum of received rewards. It may be computed with a discount factor to weight immediate rewards more heavily than future rewards.

children.name: Average Reward
children.short_definition: Average reward is a reinforcement learning objective that measures the long-run expected reward per time step obtained by an agent under a given policy, typically defined as the limit of the expected cumulative reward divided by time as the horizon grows. It is used to evaluate steady-state performance in continuing tasks without discounting.

children.name: Policy Loss
children.short_definition: Policy loss is an objective function used in reinforcement learning to quantify how well a policy’s action distribution aligns with desired behavior, typically by measuring the discrepancy between predicted actions and targets derived from rewards or advantage estimates. It guides policy optimization by providing gradients for updating policy parameters during training.

children.name: Value Function Approximation
children.short_definition: Value function approximation is a reinforcement learning technique in which a parameterized function is used to estimate the value function for states or state–action pairs when the state space is too large or continuous for exact representation. It enables generalization across states by learning the mapping from state features to expected cumulative rewards.

children.name: Mean Squared TD Error
children.short_definition: Mean Squared TD Error is a loss function in reinforcement learning that measures the expected squared difference between the predicted value function and the temporal-difference target. It quantifies the discrepancy between current value estimates and bootstrapped estimates based on observed rewards and subsequent state values.

children.name: Bellman Error
children.short_definition: Bellman error is the discrepancy between the two sides of the Bellman equation, measuring how well a value function satisfies the recursive relationship defined by expected immediate rewards and discounted future values. It is commonly used as an objective or diagnostic quantity in reinforcement learning to assess and optimize value function approximations.

children.name: Symbolic AI
children.short_definition: Symbolic AI is an approach to artificial intelligence that represents knowledge using explicit symbols and applies formal rules, logic, and inference mechanisms to reason and solve problems.

children.name: Rule-based Systems
children.short_definition: Rule-based systems are artificial intelligence systems that encode knowledge as explicit production rules and use an inference mechanism to apply these rules to a set of facts in order to derive conclusions or determine actions.

children.name: Production Rules
children.short_definition: Production rules are a formal knowledge representation used in artificial intelligence, consisting of condition–action pairs that specify actions to be taken when certain conditions are satisfied. They are commonly used in rule-based systems and expert systems to model reasoning and decision-making processes.

children.name: Inference
children.short_definition: Inference is the computational process of deriving conclusions, predictions, or decisions from data using a trained model, rules, or probabilistic reasoning. In artificial intelligence, it refers to applying learned parameters or logical structures to new inputs to produce outputs.

children.name: Soundness
children.short_definition: Soundness is a property of a formal system, model, or inference procedure whereby every statement it derives is semantically valid with respect to its intended interpretation or specification. In artificial intelligence and computer science, it guarantees that computed conclusions are correct according to the defined semantics.

children.name: Completeness
children.short_definition: Completeness is a property of an algorithm or inference system indicating that it is guaranteed to find a solution, or derive all logically entailed conclusions, whenever a solution exists for a given problem or knowledge base.

children.name: Consistency
children.short_definition: Consistency is the property of an artificial intelligence system or formal model in which its representations, inferences, or outputs do not contain internal contradictions and conform to defined rules, constraints, or assumptions across all relevant conditions.

children.name: Time Complexity
children.short_definition: Time complexity is a measure of the amount of computational time an algorithm requires as a function of the size of its input, typically expressed using asymptotic notation. It characterizes how an algorithm’s runtime grows with increasing input size under defined computational models.

children.name: Decision Support
children.short_definition: Decision support refers to computational methods and systems that assist human or organizational decision-making by collecting, processing, and analyzing data to provide relevant information, predictions, or recommendations. It focuses on enhancing judgment rather than automating decisions, often integrating analytical models, data management, and user interaction.

children.name: Correctness
children.short_definition: Correctness is the property of an algorithm, system, or program whereby its outputs and behaviors conform to its formal specification or intended requirements for all valid inputs and conditions. It is typically defined with respect to logical, mathematical, or semantic criteria rather than empirical performance alone.

children.name: Coverage
children.short_definition: Coverage is a measure of the extent to which a dataset, model, or evaluation process represents or exercises the relevant elements of a target domain. In artificial intelligence, it commonly refers to the degree to which data, features, states, or test cases adequately span the space of interest.

children.name: Consistency

children.name: Expert Systems
children.short_definition: Expert systems are computer-based systems that emulate the decision-making abilities of human experts by using a knowledge base and an inference mechanism to reason about complex problems. They apply encoded domain knowledge and logical rules to provide conclusions, recommendations, or diagnoses.

children.name: Diagnosis
children.short_definition: Diagnosis is the process of inferring the presence, nature, and causes of a condition or fault from observed data and domain knowledge. In artificial intelligence, it denotes reasoning methods that map observations to hypotheses about underlying system states, often under uncertainty.

children.name: Explainability
children.short_definition: Explainability is the degree to which the internal mechanisms and outputs of an artificial intelligence system can be understood and interpreted by humans. It concerns the ability to provide clear, accurate, and meaningful explanations of how and why a model produces specific decisions or predictions.

It focuses on enhancing judgment rather than automating decisions, often integrating analytical models, data management, and user interaction.

children.name: Logic-based Systems
children.short_definition: Logic-based systems are artificial intelligence systems that represent knowledge using formal logic and perform reasoning through logical inference rules to derive conclusions or make decisions. They emphasize explicit symbolic representations, consistency, and soundness in problem-solving and knowledge manipulation.

children.name: Propositional Logic
children.short_definition: Propositional logic is a formal logical system in which propositions are treated as atomic units that can be assigned truth values and combined using logical connectives. It provides a framework for reasoning about the truth or falsity of statements without reference to their internal structure.

children.name: Satisfiability Checking
children.short_definition: Satisfiability checking is the computational process of determining whether there exists an assignment of values to variables that makes a given logical formula or set of constraints true. It is a fundamental decision problem in logic, formal verification, and automated reasoning, commonly studied in propositional and first-order logic contexts.

children.name: First-Order Logic
children.short_definition: First-Order Logic is a formal logical system that extends propositional logic by allowing quantification over individual variables and the use of predicates, functions, and relations to express properties and relationships among objects in a domain. It provides a precise semantic and syntactic framework for reasoning about structured knowledge.

children.name: Theorem Proving
children.short_definition: Theorem proving is a branch of artificial intelligence and mathematical logic concerned with the automated or semi-automated derivation of formal proofs from axioms and inference rules within a logical system. It involves algorithms and symbolic reasoning techniques to verify the validity of propositions expressed in formal languages.

children.name: Semi-decidability
children.short_definition: Semi-decidability is a property of a decision problem for which there exists an algorithm that halts and accepts all instances with a positive answer, but may run indefinitely on instances with a negative answer. Such problems correspond to recursively enumerable sets in computability theory.

children.name: Deduction
children.short_definition: Deduction is a form of logical inference in which conclusions are derived as logically necessary consequences of given premises using formal rules of inference. In artificial intelligence, deduction is used to infer entailed facts from an explicit knowledge base within a formal logic system.

children.name: Description Logic

children.name: Consistency Checking

children.name: Soundness

children.name: Completeness

children.name: Subsumption Reasoning

children.name: Ontologies

children.name: Knowledge Validation

children.name: AI Incidents
children.short_definition: AI incidents are occurrences in which the design, deployment, or operation of an artificial intelligence system leads to unintended behavior, safety failures, legal or ethical violations, or other adverse impacts. The term is used to categorize and analyze events relevant to AI risk management, accountability, and system governance.

children.name: AIID - Artificial Intelligence Incident Database
children.short_definition: The Artificial Intelligence Incident Database (AIID) is an online, structured repository that systematically documents and categorizes real-world incidents involving the development, deployment, or use of artificial intelligence systems. It is intended to support research, risk analysis, and governance by providing standardized information on AI-related failures, harms, and unintended consequences.

children.name: MIT AI Incident Tracker
children.short_definition: The AI Incident Database is an independent online repository that systematically documents and categorizes real-world incidents involving the harmful or unintended impacts of artificial intelligence systems. It is maintained by the Responsible AI Collaborative (formerly Partnership on AI) and supported by various organizations to support analysis, accountability, and research on AI-related risks and failures.

children.name: OECD - AIM: AI Incidents and Hazards Monitor
children.short_definition: The OECD AI Incidents and Hazards Monitor (OECD‑AIM) is an online repository developed by the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development to systematically collect, categorize, and analyze publicly reported incidents and risks associated with artificial intelligence systems. It aims to support evidence-based AI governance, policy development, and risk assessment by improving transparency about real-world AI harms.

children.name: Ethical, Legal & Regulatory AI
children.short_definition: Ethical, Legal & Regulatory AI refers to the interdisciplinary domain concerned with ensuring that the development, deployment, and use of artificial intelligence systems comply with ethical principles, legal requirements, and regulatory frameworks. It addresses issues such as fairness, accountability, transparency, privacy, safety, and governance across the AI lifecycle.

children.name: BR

It encompasses the identification, assessment, mitigation, and monitoring of risks related to AI performance, security, bias, transparency, and societal impact throughout the system lifecycle.
